title: Godot will no longer accept AI-authored code contributions
titleZh: Godot 将不再接受 AI 编写的代码贡献
author: pjmlp
relevanceReason: 命中 3 个 AI/科技关键词。
summaryRaw: Godot has been suffering from a slop problem. In February, the maintainers of the open source game engine, which powers games like Slay the Spire 2 and The Case of the Golden Idol, said they were deliberating how to address a rising tide of AI slop pull requests, which had become "increasingly draining and demoralizing" for the project's code reviewers.Today, after months of discussion, the Godot Foundation and its maintainers are drawing a line in the sand. In a blog post, the Foundation announced that Godot's guidelines for contributors will soon be amended to forbid AI-authored code, pull requests submitted by AI agents, and AI-generated text in human-to-human communication."It is time for us to recognize that these problems aren’t going away and therefore we need to take steps to reduce the burden on maintainers while ensuring we still have a pipeline to mentor new contributors to become future maintainers," the Godot Foundation said.The Foundation says the pileup of Godot pull requests pending review isn't all bad: It's a sign that interest in using and contribution to Godot is increasing. But the influx of contributions authored or submitted by AI is sapping the projects' maintainers of their willingness to confront the "already tedious" work of reviewing pull requests."If your feedback on PRs is just being absorbed by a machine and not going towards mentoring a potential future maintainer, it becomes much harder to justify spending your free time on PR review," the Foundation said.As the problem becomes increasingly unsustainable, the Godot Foundation says it's in the process of updating its contribution policies, focusing on "adding barriers to low-effort slop" contributions, encouraging maintainers to review code, developing new contributors into future maintainers, and crucially, requiring that all contributions come from humans who are accountable for their code—and fixing it if it fails."AI cannot take responsibility, and we can't trust heavy users of AI to understand their code enough to fix it," the Foundation said.Keep up to date with the most important stories and the best deals, as picked by the PC Gamer team.The Foundation says we can expect Godot's contributing policy to soon include explicit rejections of AI-authored code, noting that contributors should only use AI assistance for "menial things" and must disclose its use. Additionally, the Foundation will reject any AI-generated text in human-to-human communications, saying it's "a basic principle of respect"—though it says machine translations "are still acceptable" if the original text was human-authored."Things change every day with respect to the current suite of AI tools available," the Foundation said. "We will continue taking a conservative approach in our policies towards them, but we will re-evaluate as things evolve."

Lincoln has been writing about games for 12 years—unless you include the essays about procedural storytelling in Dwarf Fortress he convinced his college professors to accept. Leveraging the brainworms from a youth spent in World of Warcraft to write for sites like Waypoint, Polygon, and Fanbyte, Lincoln spent three years freelancing for PC Gamer before joining on as a full-time News Writer in 2024, bringing an expertise in Caves of Qud bird diplomacy, getting sons killed in Crusader Kings, and hitting dinosaurs with hammers in Monster Hunter.
summaryZh: Godot has been suffering from a slop problem. In February, the maintainers of the open source game engine, which powers games like Slay the Spire 2 and The Case of the Golden Idol, said they were deliberating how to address a rising tide of AI slop pull requests, which had become "increasingly draining and demoralizing" for the project's code reviewers.Today, after months of discussion, the Godot Foundation and its maintainers are drawing a line in the sand. In a blog post, the Foundation announced that Godot's guidelines for contributors will soon be amended to forbid AI-authored code, pull requests submitted by AI agents, and AI-generated text in human-to-human communication."It is time for us to recognize that these problems aren’t going away and therefore we need to take steps to reduce the burden on maintainers while ensuring we still have a pipeline to mentor new contributors to become future maintainers," the Godot Foundation said.The Foundation says the pileup of Godot pull requests pending review isn't all bad: It's a sign that interest in using and contribution to Godot is increasing. But the influx of contributions authored or submitted by AI is sapping the projects' maintainers of their willingness to confront the "already tedious" work of reviewing pull requests."If your feedback on PRs is just being absorbed by a machine and not going towards mentoring a potential future maintainer, it becomes much harder to justify spending your free time on PR review," the Foundation said.As the problem becomes increasingly unsustainable, the Godot Foundation says it's in the process of updating its contribution policies, focusing on "adding barriers to low-effort slop" contributions, encouraging maintainers to review code, developing new contributors into future maintainers, and crucially, requiring that all contributions come from humans who are accountable for their code—and fixing it if it fails."AI cannot take responsibility, and we can't trust heavy users of AI to understand their code enough to fix it," the Foundation said.Keep up to date with the most important stories and the best deals, as picked by the PC Gamer team.The Foundation says we can expect Godot's contributing policy to soon include explicit rejections of AI-authored code, noting that contributors should only use AI assistance for "menial things" and must disclose its use. Additionally, the Foundation will reject any AI-generated text in human-to-human communications, saying it's "a basic principle of respect"—though it says machine translations "are still acceptable" if the original text was human-authored."Things change every day with respect to the current suite of AI tools available," the Foundation said. "We will continue taking a conservative approach in our policies towards them, but we will re-evaluate as things evolve."

comments.author: TomasBM
comments.textRawHtml:
It's a fair policy. Getting those verbose, AI-authored walls of text is very annoying, especially when you're expected to thoroughly review it. It's like a denial-of-service attack on the human mind. I can only imagine how frustrating this can get in open projects that get a lot of contributions.

However, I don't think this will discourage AI-based coding at all. In fact, I see two potential outcomes of these policies:

- Negative: Submitters just add stylistic markers to make their accounts and output seem human-generated. This is like syntactic sugar: the core content and the size of contributions stay the same, but the style gets quirkier.

- Positive: Submitters actually provide to-the-point, no-bullshit commits and comments - "here's the code, here's why I made that change, here are the effects of that change". Even if AI-generated, these small contributions may become much easier to verify & validate. We may even see some standardization in terms of what qualifies as an appropriately sized contribution, what requires more thorough review (e.g., adding unverified dependencies), etc.

I personally wouldn't care if it was AI-generated or not, as long as the content fit the latter category.
comments.textZhHtml:
这是一项公平的政策。获取那些由人工智能编写的冗长的文本墙非常烦人，尤其是当您需要彻底检查它时。这就像对人类思想的拒绝服务攻击。我只能想象，在获得大量贡献的开放项目中，这会多么令人沮丧。

但是，我认为这根本不会阻碍基于人工智能的编码。事实上，我看到这些政策的两个潜在结果：

- 负面：提交者只需添加风格标记即可使他们的帐户和输出看起来像是人类生成的。这就像语法糖：核心内容和贡献大小保持不变，但风格变得更古怪。

- 积极：提交者实际上提供了切中要害、没有废话的提交和评论 - “这是代码，这是我进行更改的原因，这是更改的效果”。即使是人工智能生成的，这些小的贡献也可能变得更容易验证和验证。我们甚至可能会看到一些标准化，例如什么是适当大小的贡献，什么需要更彻底的审查（例如，添加未经验证的依赖项）等。

我个人并不关心它是否是人工智能生成的，只要内容符合后一类即可。

children.author: ivorius
children.textRawHtml:
> - Negative: Submitters just add stylistic markers to make their accounts and output seem human-generated. This is like syntactic sugar: the core content and the size of contributions stay the same, but the style gets quirkier.

From my experience reviewing, most contributors never read the policies, especially those making a "quick AI PR". I don't expect the new policy to change this much.

> Positive: Submitters actually provide to-the-point, no-bullshit commits and comments

That would be a dream.
children.textZhHtml:
> - 负面：提交者只需添加风格标记即可使他们的帐户和输出看起来像是人类生成的。这就像语法糖：核心内容和贡献大小保持不变，但风格变得更加古怪。

根据我的审查经验，大多数贡献者从未阅读过这些政策，尤其是那些制作“快速人工智能公关”的人。我不认为新政策会有这么大的改变。

>积极的：提交者实际上提供了切中要害、没有废话的提交和评论

那将是一个梦想。

children.author: QuantumNomad_
children.textRawHtml:
> From my experience reviewing, most contributors never read the policies, especially those making a "quick AI PR". I don't expect the new policy to change this much.

True. At least with a policy about it, the project maintainers can unilaterally close such PRs without further internal or external discussion on any case-by-case basis.
children.textZhHtml:
> 根据我的审核经验，大多数贡献者从未阅读过这些政策，尤其是那些制作“快速 AI PR”的人。我预计新政策不会有这么大的改变。

确实如此。至少有了相关政策，项目维护者可以单方面关闭此类 PR，而无需根据具体情况进行进一步的内部或外部讨论。

children.author: maybewhenthesun
children.textRawHtml: Dingdingding, we have a winner. The main use of such a policy is to be able to just close those giant wall-of-text PRs and have something to point to when people start to scream it's not fair.
children.textZhHtml: 叮叮叮，我们赢了。这种政策的主要用途是能够关闭那些巨大的文字墙 PR，并在人们开始尖叫这不公平时有一些东西可以指出。

children.author: mcphage
children.textRawHtml:
> when people start to scream it's not fair

Or LLMs, as we have seen.
children.textZhHtml:
>当人们开始尖叫时，这不公平

或者法学硕士，正如我们所见。

children.author: chrisjj
children.textRawHtml: Or, who knows, the "AI" might gain sufficient intelligence to read the policy...
children.textZhHtml: 又或者，谁知道呢，“AI”可能会变得足够聪明，自己去读政策……

children.author: julianlam
children.textRawHtml: Then we'll amend the policy to instruct LLMs to author PRs as Fred Flintstone, yabba dabba doo!
children.textZhHtml: 那我们就修改政策，指示大语言模型以弗雷德·弗林斯通的口吻来写PR，雅巴达巴嘟！

children.author: tayo42
children.textRawHtml: Why is a policy necessary. you were never entitled to have your pr merged in the first place? If pr wasn't reviewable pre AI I'd expect it to be closed or ignored too
children.textZhHtml: 为什么需要一项政策。你从一开始就没有权利合并你的公关吗？如果公关在人工智能之前不可审查，我希望它也被关闭或忽略

children.author: the_hoser
children.textRawHtml: The policy isn't necessary to close the PR.  The policy just helps to shut down the ensuing discussion after closing the PR.  It helps in quickly dealing with well-meaning onlookers asking for clarification when you block PRs from the account.
children.textZhHtml: 关闭PR并不需要这个政策。这个政策只是在关闭PR后，帮助平息随之而来的讨论。当你封禁某个账号提交的PR时，它能帮你快速应对那些出于好意、要求澄清的旁观者。

children.author: tayo42
children.textRawHtml: Still, your not even entitled to a discussion though.
children.textZhHtml: 不过，你甚至都没有资格要求展开讨论。

children.author: bluGill
children.textRawHtml:
Before AI a large pull request was enough effort to make that you could assume good faith work on the part of everyone. Likely even if it is bad for architectural reasons it is solving an itch other users have and it is reasonable for them to want an explanation why you refused someone who made this much effort. And since the effort required meant it didn't happen often it wasn't a big deal to provide that.

These days large PRs are easy to create and so humans need to shut them down.
children.textZhHtml:
在AI出现之前，提交一个大型PR需要付出足够的努力，因此你可以假定所有人都是出于善意在工作。很可能即使它因为架构原因而不够好，但它解决了其他用户的痛点，他们要求你解释为什么拒绝一个付出这么多努力的人，这也是合情合理的。而且由于所需的工作量很大，这种情况并不常发生，所以提供解释也不是什么难事。

如今，大型PR很容易生成，因此人类需要将其拦截下来。

children.author: LordDragonfang
children.textRawHtml:
Whether or not someone is entitled to something has very little bearing on whether someone believes they are entitled to something (and are willing to waste everyone's time to make a stink about it). Having clear rules to point to, even after the fact, is surprisingly effective in mitigating that.

Or, put more bluntly, your belief in what people ought to feel entitled to has no bearing on what they do believe, and policy needs to address the latter, not the former.
children.textZhHtml:
某人是否确实有权获得某物，与某人是否认为自己有权获得某物（并愿意浪费大家的时间为此大做文章）几乎毫无关系。有明确的规则可以援引，即使在事后，也能出人意料地有效缓解这一问题。

或者，说得更直白一点，你对于人们应该觉得自己有权获得什么的看法，与他们实际的想法毫无关系，而政策需要解决的是后者，而不是前者。

children.author: jononor
children.textRawHtml: Prior to AI making a PR involved considerable effort from a human. So the default position for many open source projects was that it deserved some level of attention for the effort. Even if many projects in practice would struggle to review every PR. But with AI tools this dynamic has shifted dramatically - many PRs have basically zero effort been put into it. Additionally there are many more of them, and often way bigger also.
children.textZhHtml: 在AI出现之前，提交一个PR需要人类付出相当多的精力。因此，许多开源项目的默认态度是，它理应得到一定程度的关注，以回报这份付出。即使实际上许多项目很难审阅每一个PR。但随着AI工具的出现，这种动态发生了巨大变化——许多PR基本上没有投入任何精力。此外，PR的数量大幅增加，而且往往规模也大得多。

children.author: whateverboat
children.textRawHtml: But now with AI, this should be "easier" for some definition of easy. In the sense that in the past, this might have taken 15 minutes to write, now with AI, this can take 5 minutes to write by first getting AI to produce a summary and then using human judgement to make it better. So, it's a good idea now to actually demand the dream.
children.textZhHtml: 但现在有了人工智能，对于简单的某些定义来说，这应该变得“更容易”。从某种意义上说，在过去，这可能需要 15 分钟才能写完，而现在有了 AI，这可能需要 5 分钟才能写完，首先让 AI 生成摘要，然后使用人类判断来使其更好。所以，现在真正要求实现梦想是个好主意。

children.author: ivorius
children.textRawHtml:
If people knew how to get AI to write terse, focused summaries, sure, that might help. I haven't seen many that do (well, ignoring the toupee fallacy).

Though the most important aspect is that we need to know the motivation and thought process, and all AI can do is fabricate a 'plausible' one.
children.textZhHtml:
如果人们知道如何让人工智能编写简洁、重点突出的摘要，当然，这可能会有所帮助。我还没有看到很多这样做的人（好吧，忽略假发谬误）。

尽管最重要的方面是我们需要了解动机和思维过程，而人工智能所能做的就是制造一个“合理的”动机。

children.author: adalacelove
children.textRawHtml:
Reading AI PRs reminds me of Monty Python's holy grenade:

"And the Lord spake, saying, ''First shalt thou take out the Holy Pin. Then shalt thou count to three, no more, no less. Three shall be the number thou shalt count, and the number of the counting shall be three. Four shalt thou not count, neither count thou two, excepting that thou then proceed to three. Five is right out. Once the number three, being the third number, be reached, then lobbest thou thy Holy Hand Grenade of Antioch towards thy foe, who, being naughty in My sight, shall snuff it.'
children.textZhHtml:
阅读AI提交的PR让我想起了巨蟒剧团里的“神圣手榴弹”：

“主说：‘首先，你要拔出神圣的插销。然后，你要数到三，不多不少。三就是你要数的数字，数的数字就是三。不要数四，也不要数二，除非你接着数到三。五绝对不行。一旦数到三，也就是第三个数字，你就把安提阿的神圣手榴弹扔向你的敌人，他在我眼中是个恶棍，必将毙命。’”

children.author: dsign
children.textRawHtml: I wouldn't mind reading that and having a good chuckle while processing an MR, as long as the comment had been crafted by a person. But now I think writing in grunts is gonna become the thing. "pete? you good boy? we won't hire you/ paper you gave HR girl with older gigs? remember it pete my boy? too many words/ words were too long/ dots you used dots/ you scratched long dash with knife, but baby saw scratch/we don't ai pete/ask if they have job in next cave/good luck."
children.textZhHtml: 如果评论是真人写的，我在处理MR时读到这些并哈哈大笑也不介意。但现在我觉得，用含糊的咕哝式语言写作要成风潮了。“皮特？你是个好小伙？我们不录用你/你给HR女孩看的关于以前工作的文件？记得吗皮特我的好小伙？字太多了/字太长了/你用了点号/你用刀划了破折号，但宝贝看到了划痕/我们不用AI皮特/问问他们下个山洞里有没有工作/祝好运。”

children.author: lokar
children.textRawHtml:
I have spent many years reviewing and editing design and product documentation from more Jr engineers and product managers.

It was a constant struggle to get them to be concise, one I mostly lost.
children.textZhHtml:
我花了很多年时间审阅和编辑初级工程师和产品经理撰写的设计和产品文档。

让他们写得简洁一直是一场艰苦的斗争，而且我大多以失败告终。

children.author: chrisjj
children.textRawHtml:
> If people knew how to get AI to write terse, focused summaries...

... then flooded maintainers would be doing it.
children.textZhHtml:
> 如果人们知道如何让AI写出简洁、重点突出的摘要……

……那被淹没在PR中的维护者们早就这么干了。

children.author: ray_kay777
children.textRawHtml: I've found that the instructions "be extremely concise" gets me much closer to output that's actually sensible/helpful rather than another wall of text.
children.textZhHtml: 我发现，使用“极其简洁”这样的指令，能让我得到更合理、更有帮助的输出，而不是又一篇长篇大论。
